title: Engagement runtime — magic-link sessions and the admin inbox
problem: On its own the portal is a brochure: a visitor who fills the intake form and leaves loses everything, the consultant has no inbox of work in flight, and there is no shared surface for the two parties to exchange messages. The engagement runtime is the layer that makes the rest of the portal real. A visitor signs in with a one-time email link, their intake becomes a persistent engagement they can return to from any device, the consultant gets an admin inbox of every engagement in flight, and both parties exchange messages on the same page. The one-active-plus-one-triage capacity rule becomes something the system can actually enforce, because there are now real engagement records to count.
analysis: The runtime turns the portal's static patterns into a live system. There are no passwords: a visitor enters an email, receives a one-time link, and clicking it establishes a signed session held in a cookie. Each engagement is one record — created from an intake submission, owned by the visitor's email, carrying its intake brief, a status, a message thread, and attachments. The consultant signs in with the same email-link mechanism; an allowlist of admin addresses is the only thing that distinguishes the admin view, so there is one authentication surface to secure rather than two. The admin inbox lists every engagement in flight; the session page is the shared surface where either party reads the brief, follows the status, and posts messages. Nothing is real-time by design — a page refreshes its data when it is opened or comes back into focus, never via push — because a real-time channel would create the always-available expectation the practice exists to refuse.

decisions.decision: Magic-link sign-in — no passwords, no third-party identity providers
decisions.rationale: It is the lowest-friction identity for a one-engagement-at-a-time practice: enter an email, click a link. No password to remember, no OAuth scopes, no vendor lock-in. It also fits the async-first contract — the visitor consents to email as the medium at the door.
decisions.recommendation: Keep links single-use and short-lived, and rate-limit how often they can be requested for an email or from an address.

decisions.decision: The admin role is an email allowlist, not a separate authentication flow
decisions.rationale: The consultant signs in exactly as a visitor does; the only difference is a server-side check of the address against an allowlist on admin routes. One authentication mechanism means one security surface to reason about.
decisions.recommendation: Treat the server-side allowlist check as the source of truth; any client-side admin hint is a convenience, never a control.

decisions.rationale: Each engagement record corresponds to exactly one current or potential piece of work, which keeps the admin inbox a list of real engagements rather than a generic CRM and keeps the capacity cap cleanly countable.
decisions.recommendation: A visitor who wants to propose a second project starts a second intake; do not overload one record with multiple briefs.

decisions.decision: Polling on focus — never push, never real-time
decisions.rationale: A real-time channel is exactly the always-available expectation the practice is built to amputate. Refreshing data when a page is opened or refocused gives 'fresh on next visit' semantics, which matches the async-only contract for both parties.
decisions.recommendation: If anyone asks why updates are not live, point at the contract — live updates would oblige the other party to be available.

decisions.decision: A small, hand-rolled backend — no auth library, no ORM
decisions.rationale: Signed-cookie sessions and direct SQL keep the runtime small enough to read end to end. Adding a backend, authentication, and a database bends the boring-tech mandate, but each individual piece is kept to the smallest surface area that works.
decisions.recommendation: Resist adding an ORM, an auth library, or a state-management library until the runtime has been stable in production for a good while.

implementation: The backend runs as Cloudflare Pages Functions over a Cloudflare D1 database, with one-time link emails sent through Resend. Authentication (functions/_lib/auth.ts, functions/api/auth/*) issues a hashed single-use token, emails it, and on verification sets a signed session cookie plus a CSRF cookie; link requests are rate-limited per email and per address. Engagements live in a sessions table with a messages table and a magic-link-token table alongside it; the intake-draft store, attachments, payments, and vouches are adjacent tables. The session endpoints (functions/api/sessions/*) list a visitor's own engagements or, for an admin, all of them; create one (subject to the capacity cap); transition its status; and carry its message thread. On the client, src/pages/SessionPage.tsx is the shared engagement surface — intake summary, status timeline, message thread, attachments — used by the visitor for their own engagement and by the admin for any; src/pages/AdminInbox.tsx is the admin's list of every engagement with its triage SLA; src/pages/Login.tsx requests a link; src/pages/MePortal.tsx is the signed-in visitor's dashboard, and includes account deletion and a data-access page for Quebec privacy-law compliance.
successCriteria:
- A visitor can save an intake, leave, and return on any device via a one-time email link with their engagement intact
- The session page shows the intake brief, the status, and a message thread, and both parties can post to it
- The admin inbox lists every engagement in flight with its triage deadline
- Admin-only routes refuse any signed-in address that is not on the allowlist
- One-time links are single-use, short-lived, and rate-limited
- The capacity cap is enforced from real engagement counts
- Nothing in the runtime creates a real-time or always-available expectation
userGuide: As a visitor: fill the intake form, then claim it by requesting a one-time sign-in link at /login. The link lands you on your dashboard at /me, where each engagement opens to its own page — the intake brief at the top, the status, and a message thread. Post a message whenever you like; the consultant replies on the same page, with no notifications by design. You can return from any device by requesting a fresh link with the same email, delete your account from the dashboard, and review your stored data at /me/data. As the consultant: sign in with the same link flow; if your address is on the admin allowlist, /admin/inbox lists every engagement, sorted by triage urgency. Open one to read the brief, reply, change status, or attach files.
knownLimitations: No real-time updates by design — a reply appears when the other party next opens or refocuses the page
Multi-admin support is only the email allowlist — adding a co-admin means editing the allowlist
Message bodies are plain text — there is no rich-text editor or markdown rendering
Engagements are never hard-deleted from the runtime — they are soft-statused and soft-deleted, which suits a text-and-attachment history
The whole backend runs on one hosting provider's platform — moving providers would be a non-trivial port, an accepted trade-off for the cost and the data-residency fit

statusHistory.reason: Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/SessionPage.feature.json. The runtime has shipped: magic-link auth, the sessions and messages tables, the admin inbox, attachments, and the privacy-law data pages are all live.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Initial fill ahead of build — the dynamic surface that makes intake, the status canvas, and the capacity cap render against real visitor engagements.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Code-first overhaul: co-located next to SessionPage.tsx, anchored the feature on a real componentFile, added liveUrl, and rewrote from the shipped runtime — dropping the stale limitations for file uploads, account deletion, and data export, which now all exist.
